transcript.transcript: Hi, I'm Tom Brown and I'm volunteering with the Emmet Orchards Oral History Project. Today's Monday, March 2nd, 2026. I'm in Beaverton, Oregon, in the home of my second cousin, Cecelia Tollison, and with her husband, Neal nearby. Um, and today we're going to talk about, um, your early life in Emmet and our heritage from the orchards of that place. Um, you're going to have an opportunity to, uh, share some of your life experience and memories. Um, this oral history is conducted with the support of the Idaho Historical Society as part of Idaho's commemoration of America 250. Uh, Cecilia, thank you for letting me into your home. Uh, lots of warm feelings right now. And, um, uh, thank you for your time today. This interview, uh, is going to allow you to share those memories. And so before we jump into the questions, do you give permission to record and preserve these stories? Yes, I do. And, uh, could you please state and spell your first and last name? Cecilia Tollisen c e c i l i a Tollisen t o l l i s e n. Thank you. Thank you. Cecilia. Um, we overlapped our time of childhood in Emmett. Uh, I always knew, uh, my great uncle Frank and great aunt Cecilia. And I think you were aware of my grandparents, Hazel and Allen Brown. Uh, we saw each other occasionally in childhood, but it was after I graduated from college that we really became close. And it was because you lived for a period of time with your grandmother in Emmett. How did that come to be? How long were you there? What were you doing there? Well, um, I had gone to school in Spokane, Washington, without an idea of what my major was going to be. So when I finished my sophomore year, it was like, don't spend any more money until you figure out what you're going to do. And so I was taking time to to think about it, pray about it, decide, I was deciding between environmental education or nursing. And I also, um, my heritage with my grandmother, uh, Cecilia Wills. I was named after her. Uh, was that she, uh, made homemade everything homemade pickles, jams, jellies, pies, mincemeat, sauerkraut, all of this. And I wanted to learn how to do it. So I decided that I would spend this time that I was trying to decide what to do, uh, going and living with her. So I lived with her for a summer and a full fall, um, trying to follow her around and figure out and take notes on, uh, how she did all of the homemade food that she made, including her hand-dipped chocolate candies. All right. And what were, um, some, uh, things that you learned from her that that summer you wanted to learn how to do something? Well, I learned that, um, she could not tell me what she did. She had to show me, uh, she didn't measure most things. And so after the first time, I'd be trying to figure out, well, how much did she use? She didn't know, so I began to realize I had to reverse engineer like she'd put it in. And then I would go and measure how much she put in. Um, and I just had to write down as she was going because she lost track. If she tried to tell me what she was doing, she lost track as she was going. Um. So those were the big things. So I can imagine you asking her a lot of questions. Yeah. Did she like answering? No, she didn't like answering. Which is why I finally had to just observe and write down. Okay. And I did find out later that when I compare recipes for things with my siblings, everybody has a slightly different recipe, that ingredients are the same, but the measurements are all just a little bit different. Okay. Now, um, uh, I think you've just mentioned this, but we'll we'll repeat a bit. It was in this time period you decided to pursue nursing? Yes. And why? What was. Where did your interest in nursing come from? And I know there's two parts to that story, but at the time, what was pushing you toward nursing? Um. I was. Well, I had recently been with a friend who had had a baby, and she had allowed me to be with her in the process of her giving birth. And I absolutely loved it. It was such a privilege and an honor, and it was very exciting to me. Mhm. Um, so that was a big push towards the nursing part. I really wasn't so much that I wanted to be a nurse. I wanted to be a labor and delivery nurse. Uh, the other one that I'd been considering was environmental education, because I had been an outdoor school counselor numerous times teaching kids about the environment, in the environment. And I loved it. Um, Um, but I think in the end I decided it was more, uh, both worthwhile and profitable as a money making career if I was going to have a career to do nursing. Mhm. At the time, as you were making these decisions about how you would continue your education, did your grandmother have input for you? Not that I recall. She, she was happy to be with you and not, not super directive. Uh, no. She, she in my memory anyway, I don't remember her being directive at all. Okay. Yeah. Yeah. So, um, that was a wonderful time. There was a piece of furniture in your grandmother's house. Um, and I guess we'll back up to to. We're going to show a piece of furniture that belonged to your grandfather. But let's talk about that childhood sorrow, of his, um, death. Uh, what do you remember about that? Um, I really don't remember very little. I just remember my grandfather being a quiet presence, um, in our lives. I, I remember him taking care of cows, and I remember, I mean, I don't know, do I have to tell the tell my cow story? So the the biggest memories I have of my grandfather, uh, were he had a few cows, and the barn was not right next to the house. You had to go a little, little ways. And because they were dairy cows. And he had milk. Uh, they he milked them. And then they had these big, um, milk crates, not crates. That's not. The word. Milk cans. Milk cans, which are actually on my front and back porch right now. Samples of those milk cans. Um, they were really heavy. And so he had to take the pickup down to the barn. So I remember times when I was in early grade school going down to the barn with him. He's milking the cows. The milk is going into these, these huge cans. And then he would lift them back up into the pickup, drive them back up to the house, and then the ditch ran right along. My parents, my grandparents, um, the road, Wills Road. And there was an opening there, and he set those big cans into the water there. I can't remember what the thing was called. It's a box that the water flows through and you divert it to different. Yeah. And parts. And it was like a it was like a cement. Yes. It was like the size of a sauna or something like that. Um, full of cold water that was running through. And he put the cans in there and then the milk truck would come by and. Wow. And collect them. That's a wonderful memory. Yeah. They also sometimes did something right. When you went to my grandparents house, there was, uh, this this landing where you go upstairs or down, and there was a faucet there inside the house. And at some point he did something with cans sometime there. But I never knew what that was. It was just this mysterious faucet right there where there was no sink or anything. But I remember going to the barn with my my grandfather. I remember him letting us ride on the tailgate of the pickup, and those were pre-seatbelt days, so we weren't expecting to be in the seatbelt, but we didn't often get to sit loose on the end of the tailgate, so he would let us do that as we went down to the barn. And I can remember butchering and making sausage with them. What, uh, what what, uh, livestock was butchered. Cows. Cows? Yeah. Okay. No no no no, actually it was it was both cows and pigs. Okay. And I have the I have the most vivid memory of pigs because of sausage. Okay. Wonderful sausage. Cleaning out the intestine casings, which we thought was so gross but so fascinating. Yeah. Medical students. Yeah, yeah, yeah. So I so am I getting back to my grandfather. I just remember him as being this quiet, lovely presence. And I remember sitting with him on the lawn in the summertime, and they had these overstuffed lawn chairs kind of things. And he would my grandmother would make lemonade and, and he would say, "lemonade made in the shade made by an old maid." That was. That was the thing he would say. Would say about my grandmother bringing us lemonade. And. Sitting there. What did you learn about this piece of folk art? So this, this piece of furniture made out of the horns of some kind of cattle was my was in my grandparents' house. And what my understanding was that it came from my, um, from when my grandfather was a kid and it came from his house. In fact, this chair that you're sitting in, Tom, used to be covered in this material. Okay. Um, before it, it kind of fell apart. We fought over who got to sit in this chair. All right. This is the chair. I'm going to add a tiny bit of history. Um, this was a gift to your grandfather from his grandfather, David Comfort Wills, who made this kind of furniture for all of his grandchildren. Really? My grandmother had a similar chair. And, um, um, I've, the DeClark family in the big old house at Main Street and John's um, where Belle Wills DeClark was a sister to our great grandfather, James Albert Wills and, um, his brothers Charlie, Billy, Irvey. And I've seen photographs of the DeClark children sitting on the porch in chairs like this, made by David Comfort of Shenandoah, Iowa. It makes you wonder how many of those there are around. Yes, we still have one in, um, our branch of the family. Sadly, we've lost most. But one remains. And this is still a strong chair. I mean, our our, uh, great nieces and nephews use this chair just this winter. That's fantastic. Um. Other stories. Thoughts about your grandfather? Um, I, I don't have any of my own memories about his, uh. Well, the only other memory I have is going down to the barn with him one time, coming back, walking back up with him and reaching for the, um, the barbed wire fence, um, not realizing that it was on. And so I touched the wire fence and I couldn't let go, but it was very low voltage fence, and he was able to immediately remove my hand from it and unhook it. I don't know if it was in that order, because I don't think it was a strong, uh, strong charge. But it was definitely a lesson of, remember, don't touch the wires. You can't tell if they're on or not. So you just mentioned you've heard others talk about your grandfather. And I'm really curious about if you and your father, uh, Frank Robert Wills, talked about his childhood or the influence of his dad on his life. Work ethic? Yeah. I mean, I don't I don't think we've talked about it. We've I think we've seen it. Mhm. Um, I, he died when I was like 7, or 8 years old. Um, so I don't have a lot of direct memories and I don't recall having conversations about my grandfather. Mhm. Um I do, I do know that there's family recordings um about different things that happened. Um, in terms of his death and how challenging that was for my grandmother. Um, it was a terrible blow, uh, to her. Um. And your grandmother managed the farm as a widow. She moved forward. Yes. Talk about her and her work, particularly with the orchard that she continued. Um, so it was always amazing to me that my grandmother, uh, was so incredibly busy. All she was always working and she could always put and she always did put an amazing meal on the table. Mhm. And that was three meals a day. And so we, we when we lived in, in, in Emmett uh, we always had Sunday dinner at her house, and if my parents ever went out of town, we would go to my grandmother's and just stay with her. If we were with my grandmother and we were going to school at the time, then our lunch box was considerably fuller. If we had been at my grandmother's house when we went to school, and it was in those days where you went, you went to school, we went to the little Sacred Heart School in Emmett, and, uh, you ate your lunch at your desk, and then you went outside. And when we had been at my grandmother's house, and we called [her] Pamommy, if we'd been at Pamommy's house, we were the last person out to recess because she loaded our lunch box up with everything, even like a pickles to go with your sandwich. So you always. It took a long time to eat, and it was so good. And she was working usually full time at the packing house, so she would get up very early in the morning, work in her garden, make us a hot breakfast, wake us up to to eat that hot, hot breakfast, give us a couple chores to do, which might be hang the laundry on the line or something pretty easy like that. And I have come across so many letters now going through family history. We wrote a lot of letters to my parents and my grandmother. That whole letter writing thing, I think is such a bonding, a bonding thing. Um, but you're referring to the transition that happened when we had to sell my grandmother's place. Um, which was hard, but necessary. And I learned today that that Mark was the administrator of her estate. He was the executor of the estate, and. But we all, almost all of us, we spent a lot of time going through her house, her, her barns, her sheds, all, you know, the whole family was involved. All of the siblings were involved in doing that. And then when it sold, and Mark was in his early 30s when this happened. So he had a big job. Um, but when we were able to sell the farm and we were thankfully sold it to people who were going to use it for horses, and so we were happy that somebody was going to actually, they weren't farming, but at least they were doing the that, um, that life. Um, we brought all of her possessions, uh, to Portland, the Portland area, because we were we were all living in the Pacific Northwest from Seattle to Portland. All that isn't true. My sister was living in new Jersey at the time. So 14 years there? Yeah. So Portland was a little bit of a hub, but I had just, uh, with, with the, um, uh, help of, of inheriting from my grandmother was had been able to purchase a home, put a down payment on a home and I had an empty house. So we brought all of my grandmother's possessions in a U-Haul from Emmett to, uh, to Beaverton. And we made lists of all of her things because they were all her her China, her her milk cans, her furniture. It was all so absolutely, intimately familiar to each one of us. Um, her Hummel collection, her teacup collection. Um, and you're taking this incredibly full life. Her furniture for furniture, which we are seeing my living room, the the oak rocker over here that sat in her spray shed for years. And I had it. It refinished after I actually everything was, you know well beloved my my husband sitting in a chair. That was what we called the king chair. You're sitting in the Queen chair. Okay, this was the paper reading chair. The lamp. The the the hurricane lamp, the purple lamp. So, um, uh, the the dining room table, I mean, really, my siblings can come here and they can just see that that there's a, there's a pie cupboard over in the corner there. That pie cupboard came out of a derelict house on the Kelly place, the apple orchard, and we rescued it from that place and refinished it to use as a pie cupboard. Wow, wow wow, wow. Um, uh, your grandmother Cecelia's namesake. Um, that's preserving the lot. Yeah, yeah. Yeah. Yeah. But we were all very unified in in wanting to make sure I can. I can remember, you know, there's discussions about money and possessions and who's going to get what. And you know, who needs who needs a bedroom set and who needs furniture and all of this stuff. And I remember we were writing letters to each other and figure out how we were going to do this. And my sister Tess said, the most important thing about this is that we all love and care in our relationship with each other. She said it would be better to sell it all and have nobody have it and divide it evenly than to fight and to have, uh, to bring to to have a break in our relationship because of these things. Every single thing that we are inheriting is a gift from her, and we need to treat it as a gift. And her saying that, I think, set the tone for the generosity of heart in each one of us to say, I may not get every piece that I want. I will get the things that are most important to my heart, and I will be able to rejoice that someone in the family has them. And I think we accomplished that sense of being able to to love one another and not let inheritance, you know, divide us. Mhm. Mhm. Even though it's looking like I got the lion's share but. Um it was a good process. Right. Wow. Um, we haven't mentioned your mom yet. Who. Who raised five kids. Um, whose husband left early and who developed an amazing career of her own. Can you talk a bit about her and her influence in your whole family? I would specifically. Um, she was, uh, born just out of Washington, D.C.. Yeah. And I know that, um, she joyously took each grandchild on a trip to her hometown. Yeah. Yeah. Mhm. Um, so has anybody talked about her Emmett time? Um. Um, yes. And what do you have to say about her? Well, I just wanted to say that none of us realized this until we were much older, but she's essentially an only child raising five from the city. Raising five kids in a tight rural community where she is a distinct outsider. And she's city, and she's trying to fit into a family that has deep roots in this valley and has a life completely different. And she they did not get her and she had to, I think, prove her worth over and over and over again. And we were completely unaware of that struggle until we were older and looked back because she did it beautifully and graciously. She is a strong extrovert. She is a formidable advocate of of um, of education and opportunity. Um, and so probably somebody mentioned one of her ways to get out of the house and to meet people was to sell World Book Encyclopedias. I've gotten to tell my little story. Yeah. So so she she sold World Book Encyclopedia. She met other people with children. She was able to, I think, in kind of a way, say, yes, I'm a housewife. And, uh, you know, a farmer's daughter-in-law, but I have a little space outside of that to be an individual and to sort of promote some of her beliefs. She was also, you know, Cub leader for Boy Scouts. She was a Girl Scout leader. We met at the Legion Hall in Emmett and had our Girl Scout, uh, troops there. Um, she became as as we all got into school, she became involved in the, um, religious education at our churches. And so when she saw when we were at confirmation time, by the time we had moved to Spokane and she saw that the program for kids was not kid appropriate, it really didn't meet the needs of children learners. She volunteered to work with the development of programs for, for uh, students, um, doing their sacrament of confirmation. And that led her into becoming a director of religious education for children in parishes, which eventually was a paid job. And then she eventually went back to graduate school. And her master's is a Master of Arts in creating Christian Community, Adult Christian Community. And so she took that to the level that of of of adults. And I, I think the thing I will always remember about my mom's influence is that she wasn't just good at getting people to come in and do the job. She really created an enormous amount of community wherever she went. She was a strong extrovert. She wasn't what I would call a particularly organized person in the way that I am organized. But she knew how to organize people and to to pull out people's best skills and let them go at it. Her skill was the big picture, seeing the pieces that needed to be done. And then she would find the people and say, you can do this. This is your deal. You do it. And she could set people free. Amazing, amazing. And she did that for us too. She was not a micromanager. And therefore we learned a lot by doing. Mhm. Mhm. And she gave uh your children and your nieces and nephews that travel experience to Washington, D.C.. Yeah. She, she actually did two trips with them when they were five. She took them on a short overnight trip. So it was maybe from here to the beach. Or if you were in Seattle, you got a train trip to Portland, or if you were in Portland, maybe you got a train trip to Seattle. So you had your individual time with going where you wanted to do with her when you were five. And then when they were 11, she took them to Washington, D.C.. and her one her second grandchild. Uh, Holly had already been fortunate enough to go to Washington, DC, so, uh, they got to go to Europe. They went to, uh, Italy. Huh? Yeah, I think it was Italy. Uh, short factual question. What's the German word for aunt? Tante. I have learned that that German word, perhaps from the Kreizenbeck family, is still used, um, in your family. And that it's a key descriptor of you. That is true. I think you should turn the tape off before I tell you where Tante really came from. Oh, well, you have your choice to tell us or not. Uh, it was chosen because it was the German. It was the German word. And and we have we have a hodgepodge of, of, um, heritage from both sides of the family. But the strongest thing that came through was our German. Uh, it was, uh, we didn't have any aunts on my dad's side of the family, on my mom's side of the family, we had aunts that that, uh, were challenging people. And so when we became aunts, I wanted a different word. So interesting. Yeah, I we we can fill in the gaps. That's a different that's a different video. But yeah, it was, it was I, I don't want to be called Aunt Cecilia. Uh, that because the aunt association was not completely positive. Okay. So I said I would like to be Tante Cecilia and my sisters and my sister in law bought into it, which is lovely. So I've gotten to know, uh, your two children who happen to be the youngest of your your mother's grandchildren. And for a lot of years, you were that special. We'll use the word aunt for right now. Uh, to a lot of nieces and nephews. And that you too, like your mom, got to create some pretty special experiences for the next generation. Yeah. What were some things you did? Well, I was the, um. I was the single aunt when. When my siblings were all having kids. So I had, uh, I had, I didn't I had money that I could spend because I wasn't spending them on my own kids, and I had time, and so. And I love kids. So, uh, when I bought a house, well, even before I bought a house, I always had a room for the nieces and nephews, and they would come and spend the night. So I was there. I was their adventure aunt, you know, they they that was probably for, for a lot of them. The first time they spent time away from home was going to Cecilia's house and spending the night and cooking and, uh, you know, working in the garden with me, playing with my dog. Uh, I also really enjoy camping, which was something that my my came from my dad's side of the family. His love of camping. And so, um, I for some of them, I was the introduction to camping. And so I started eating when they were as young as two years old, some of them taking him camping every summer. Mhm. Mhm. We're starting to wrap it up. I, I might come up with a few more questions but at this. Point much in orchards yet. Well what else would you like to tell us about orchards. You've talked about some work. You did the work, some of the work of your your your grandfather, your father, your grandmother. Yeah. There isn't a whole lot more to tell. Okay, okay. I'm just thinking. This is. This is the orchard. Well, it's. It's the heritage of the orchard, so. Okay, I've got another story I want to tell. Okay. It doesn't have to do with orchards, but it's the heritage of the valley. So, um, my dad grew up just the. Just up Johns Avenue from, um, the Robinson family. Okay. And so the Robinsons had a dairy? Yes. And that is where we got our milk. When we lived there, we would go to the Robinson's house and, um. As did we. Yeah. And with your big one gallon, you know, big jars, and you go in and you get your, your milk every week, and then the cream rises to the top and you skim that. So anyway, we were, uh, my, my dad was good friends with them. Had gone, Had gone to school with. I'm going to forget his name. David Robinson. David Robinson. Absolutely. And and they had kids our age. So sometimes we would go there just for fun. Mhm. Um, so one of my fun. Oh I know what we were doing. We were doing an exchange. Uh, we had gotten our milk from them. Uh, we took a bunch of apples over there and we're doing an apple press and doing apple cider in the fall one time. So the adults were doing that. The kids were playing around in the barn, and, uh, we were playing like, follow the leader in, in the barn. And so we were we scrambled up to the second floor with a hayloft was and we're running through there, and lo and behold, I came across a weak place in the floorboards and fell through with one leg. So my my body was on top and one leg was sticking down underneath, and all of my friends that I was running around chasing with all ran down the stairs so they could look up at the ceiling and see this one leg hanging down from the ceiling in the barn. And I was perfectly fine, with the exception of a few scratches on my legs. But the Robinsons didn't let kids run. Other people's kids run around in their barn. After that. I ran around in the barn with, um, Paul Robinson, son of David Robinson, who is the same age as your brother Mark. Uh, my first cousin, Allen. And me. Yeah. And Susan was exactly between, uh, myself and Lisa, and Susan and I both ended up going to the same nursing school and graduating the same time. The same nursing school became friends in college. Really. Well, a little bit before that. And our friends to this day. Um, another connection. Another connection. And they're still raising cows and producing milk and milk products. And some organic products. It's all organic. It's all organic beef and, uh, yeah, their milk. Yeah. I, um, I know that you were close to your sister, Lisa. Can you talk about her life? We're sad to have, uh, lost her. Yeah. And, um, um, she was the fourth of of of your five siblings, and, um, has two wonderful children. Yeah. Um. What can. What would you like us to know about Lisa? It's it's it's a little hard to talk about her, because I think if you give a biography of her life. Uh. There were a lot of challenges for her. Mhm. Um, I guess the thing I would really want to say about her is that she always, um, pursued truth. She pursued truth in herself. Um, she wanted to be genuine. She wanted to be honest. She pursued spiritual truth like no one I have ever met. She. If there is a God to know, I want to know that God. I want to know him accurately, genuinely, wholeheartedly. Um. And she she pursued that. So she went deep within. She went deep with other people. She went deep with God. And I have such respect and honor and and just, uh, love for her on, on every level. I was very honored to be, uh, probably her one of her biggest confidants within within the family. You know, I shared shared a lot of her struggles. Um, but I think the sum up of her life is just a person who pursued God and pursued truth, and and, uh. Well, I have so much admiration for her. I'm so glad you got to share your love for her. I've had this great opportunity this weekend to talk to the four remaining siblings, and you have brought her to life for us in a beautiful way. Um, I'm going to share a short reflection and then give you a chance for the final word. Um, something you said really struck through, um, the silos that existed as we were growing up next door separately. Um, the the Hazel and Allen Brown Ranch and the Frank and Cecilia Ranch and the different lives that were associated by, uh, family members that went to the Emmett Bible Hall, which had started in the living room of our great grandmother, Ruth Wills. Um, the your family attending Catholic school and the Kreizenbeck family being some of the probably the first members and builders of the Catholic Church in Emmett. Different silos. Um, and it's interesting that in one community, Those those separations existed. And yet the amazing things we have in common. Um, Mark was telling me today about using a wire to get the sand out of a sprinkler head. And I remembered having that job at my grandpa's orchards. We're doing the same thing at the same harvest, side by side. And here we are, uh, getting to know each other again. And it's sort of broader and deeper and recognizing an amazing common heritage. Yeah. Um, where the Wills united in Emmett, Idaho. Sister Belle married the real estate guy. DeClark moves to Emmett first. Uh, Bert, brothers Bert and Billy moved from Wyoming to Emmett a year or two later, uh, brothers Charlie and Irving and their mother, Anna Wolf Wills, moved to Emma. You had these five siblings Rejoining and uh, and the the the only children the hard work and separations. But, um, yours is a story of amazing togetherness that your parents, um, you yourselves have, have created. I just want to acknowledge that. And I want to give you the last word. What question should I have asked us? Asked or what? What do you really want to be the last word about the heritage that we share from those orchards and and generations now since then? Yeah. Well, I guess I would, I would reflect on this. You know, when you ask me, what did I know about the Browns on one side and the, you know, the Kenny Wills on the other side. And, you know, I feel, you know, uh, it makes me sad to say I didn't really have a strong awareness of them. Other. Other than getting bit by Kathy's horse, Kathy Wills's horse, one time. That's my only memory of visiting them is giving giving the horse an apple and it bites me in the shoulder, which was probably my own fault because I don't think I knew anything about horses. Uh. And knowing that there were so many connections there, and at least in the time of my youth, it didn't feel like, uh, it wasn't a relationship connection. It was. It was a known connection. Um, but the thing that I did see, uh, especially on my grandmother's side, is as different as they were. We knew that they were family. We knew we had an Uncle George and an Uncle Joe and an Uncle John and an uncle Bill. And we had, you know, we had those people. We knew we had Winnie, you know, we knew we had Winnie. Winnie Wills Winnie Wills. And we knew about Hazel. We knew we had some town cousins. So somehow, even though we didn't have the relationship, then there was that fundamental sense of family and connectedness that was very, very real. Even if it wasn't manifested for me as kind of a person who, who came and went from Emmet came and went physically. But it was really a spiritual home. That was a home thing. And so even though I don't know very many people from there. The ones I do know, like you, we have a very good connection. We had a lovely connection with my my godmother, Agnes, who did not have her own kids. And so there, because of her going to funerals, you meet the other relatives and there is that, that sense of commonality. So some something about that family connectedness must be part of what keeps our even my sibling group family together and connected, uh, we now all do live in, in the same city. Um, but even when we didn't, there is a fundamental magnet that keeps us, keeps us together. Mhm, mhm. So delightful to share our orchard heritage. Yeah. And, um, our lifelong friendship, uh, that that childhood acquaintance that got to grow and has maintained as my sisters live nearby in Vancouver, Washington, not far from Beaverton, having gotten to, uh, be, uh, at your mother's memorial. Um, okay, one more question. Uh, briefly, can you tell us what we've been doing together this weekend? Well, first of all, I want to just completely acknowledge how key you are in making us aware of our family connections because you are the historian, you're the record keeper. You're the person with the entire family tree in your head, which is so amazing to me. And you present it in such a way that is so appealing that makes you want to belong to that. So I really, hugely, hugely appreciate all of the work and all of your interest and all of your joy when you do it, because you draw us in, you really do. You make it's like, oh my goodness, this is very cool. And so, so we were just together because my family as uh, my mom passed away a year ago. And so the my siblings and I have have been working through her estate now in our 60s, the way my, my brother was the one working through my, my parents and grandmother's estate. And when we were in our 30s, um, we have worked through her estate and the final things the family photographs, the family, uh, albums, the historical records, all of the the things that you can't, you know, sell it in the estate Estates were left, and so we had just spent the weekend together at a retreat center, bringing all of those things together, with all of us there to go through them, to reminisce and to make the decisions about what's going to happen with the family records and the photographs and what to keep and what to let go. And, and, uh, and Thomas helped us in that process. Why did you invite me? Uh, well, A, I thought you thought it would think it would be fun. You were right. And, uh, also, uh, because you have a perspective and a knowledge that we don't have. So we could take out a picture and go. This is. Oh. Which side of the family is this on? Who is this person? And Tom would know. And hopefully you you also helped us hold on to things that are were better to, to hold on to because somebody in the next generation is going to want to know some of this stuff. So Yeah. I mean, we had been connected because of your doing family history work. Um, on the will side of the family pretty far back. And so it's like, well, to have a resident historian here with us, that's all to the good. Well. Thank you. That's. It was an amazing weekend. Um, I've learned so much. Um, I've interviewed, uh, Tess, Mark and you. I knew that time was a little short yesterday. I'll be, uh, interviewing Michael at a later time. Uh, but, uh, to get all of your perspectives has been very special. And you are unique. You are articulate and bright and, um, a sense of spiritual connection. That's beautiful. And it shines through. Oh, thank you so much. Thank you. I think that's something we should. All right. Thank you. Yeah. Also unique.

story.title: narrator Cecilia Tollisen interview by TBrown
story.description: The interview centers around the oral history of the Emmet Orchards and familial connections, emphasizing Cecilia's heritage and the influence of her grandmother. Cecilia shares her journey of education and career choices, with a focus on her decision to pursue nursing inspired by family connections and personal experiences. The narrative weaves through generational stories, including agriculture, familial responsibilities, and the intricate network of relatives associated with the orchards. There is a consistent theme of interconnectedness, preservation of family heritage, and the continuation of traditions in modern times.
